title: podman pod ps aborts with Error: container [...] not found in DB: no such container
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

```
❯ podman pod ps
Error: container 85ea779d81e95632061[...] not found in DB: no such container
```

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a pod and interrupt the process in the wrong moment?

**Describe the results you received:**

```
❯ podman pod ps
Error: container 85ea779d81e95632061[...] not found in DB: no such container
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

No error message. Empty list or a list containing `POD_ID **broken**` or similar would be have acceptable. Possibly with instructions on how to recover.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
❯ podman version
Version:      3.4.0
API Version:  3.4.0
Go Version:   go1.15.15
Built:        Mon Oct  4 19:52:18 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
The problem persists even after a reboot.

`sudo rm -fr .local/share/containers` "fixed" the issue.
Can  you attempt to remove the container?

> Can you attempt to remove the container?

`podman ps` did not display any container. `podman rm` claimed it did not exist.
It looks like the db got in a corrupted state somehow. The pod still exists in the DB and has containers but the container itself is not in the DB listed.

> I meant podman pod rm --all --force 

Sorry, I already deleted `~/.local/share/containers` and did not save a backup.

> Any idea how you got into that state?

I have a script that in essence does the following:

```sh
pod="$(podman pod create --name=... --add-host=... --publish=...)"
onexit() {
        podman pod rm --force "${pod}"
}
trap onexit EXIT

# BLOCK START

# Several of the following:
podman run --privileged --pod="${pod}" ...

# Then once:
podman run --detach --pod="${pod}" ...

# BLOCK END

# And then finally:
podman run --tty --interactive --pod="${pod}" --cap-add=CAP_NET_ADMIN --mount=... ...
```

I hit ^C somewhere between `BLOCK START` and `BLOCK END`.

I think you interrupted the process somewhere between `r.state.RemovePodContainers(p)` and `r.state.RemovePod(p)` here:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/97f051f65769fed32b3c497b3f484667400c66a9/libpod/runtime_pod_linux.go#L268-L358

To prevent races like that everything must happen in a single `db.Update()` call. @mheon WDYT?

I don't think that should leave us in a bad state - the pod and DB will still be internally consistent after a `RemovePodContainers`. All containers are removed entirely from the DB, including from the pod itself, so it will no longer reference them.

It almost looks as though a transaction partially committed? Not sure if that's possible...

RemovePodContainers() does not remove the pod completely from the DB
Compared to RemovePod() it is missing `podBkt.DeleteBucket(podID)` and `allPodsBkt.Delete(podID)`
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/libpod/boltdb_state.go#L3018-L3023
Yes, it doesn't, but it won't lead to this behavior. The containers of the pod will be completely removed, so the issue here cannot occur.
There's a chance this could have been https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/11774
@fdcds Any `podman rename` commands in your script?

> The containers of the pod will be completely removed, so the issue here cannot occur.

But that is the issue `podman pod ps` is failing because it will read all pods and then containers. We can the pods successfuly with `AllPods()` https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/libpod/boltdb_state.go#L3334 

after that podman pod ps will read the container for this pod here: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/pkg/domain/infra/abi/pods.go#L354

This will call `PodContainers()`   https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/libpod/boltdb_state.go#L2208

and then will fail here when it tries to get the container from the DB https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/libpod/boltdb_state.go#L2262

At least this is how I read the code

But the pod has all reference to containers it once held removed by `RemovePodContainers()` - https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/libpod/boltdb_state.go#L3144-L3150

@mheon You can reproduce with this patch
```diff
diff --git a/libpod/runtime_pod_linux.go b/libpod/runtime_pod_linux.go
index 9c6f1539f..8112ccb3d 100644
--- a/libpod/runtime_pod_linux.go
+++ b/libpod/runtime_pod_linux.go
@@ -8,6 +8,7 @@ import (
        "path"
        "path/filepath"
        "strings"
+       "time"
 
        "github.com/containers/common/pkg/config"
        "github.com/containers/podman/v3/libpod/define"
@@ -272,6 +273,9 @@ func (r *Runtime) removePod(ctx context.Context, p *Pod, removeCtrs, force bool,
                return err
        }
 
+       fmt.Println("ctl-c now")
+       time.Sleep(15 * time.Second)
+
        for volName := range ctrNamedVolumes {
                volume, err := r.state.Volume(volName)
                if err != nil && errors.Cause(err) != define.ErrNoSuchVolume {
```
Now run 
`bin/podman pod create --name test`
```
bin/podman pod rm test 
ctl-c now
^C
```
```
bin/podman pod ps
Error: container 42d0025b99053602aa3081cea6029d39fc928f0216581c95f34bebb669e64c5a not found in DB: no such container
```
I wonder if `DeleteBucket()` + `CreateBucket()` is not doing what I think it is - maybe it retains previous contents?

No that works, I was wrong where it fails.
It fails because podman pod ps tries to read the infra container here: 
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/eba281c3e0e69533427d3eb5c66c60b5ca038c1b/pkg/domain/infra/abi/pods.go#L369-L375
`InfraContainerID()` reads the id from the pod state and `p.InfraContainer()` tries to lookup this id in the container bucket and it fails.
Ahhhh. OK.

Infra container ID is in the mutable part of the DB. We can clear it immediately before we do the `RemoveAllContainers` bit. I don't think that has negative implications if we're interrupted - the pod will be in a bad state but `podman pod rm` should still work.
Is there any reason why we do not combine RemovePodContainers and RemovePod is the same function so we can use the same db.Update transaction?

We could combine them, but I originally wrote it this was to split the removal process into smaller bits that should be less likely to race if we're interrupted - we remove containers first as one discrete block, then the pod, always leaving behind a sane state.

> @fdcds Any `podman rename` commands in your script?

No.

In case that helps somehow: `strings .local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db | grep 85ea779d81e95632061` had hits containing references to `.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/...` (maybe others too, but that's what I remember right now), but the files / directory I saw in the output did not exist.
Fix should be in #12047
